sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of breast cancer requires a multidisciplinary approach tailored to the tumor's clinical stage, biological subtype, and patient-specific factors. Treatment is guided by the principles of maximizing cure while minimizing treatment-related morbidity and preserving quality of life. A multimodal strategy typically incorporates surgery, radiation therapy, and systemic therapy (endocrine, cytotoxic, targeted, or immunotherapy). The choice and sequencing of these modalities depend on the disease's extent, tumor biology (hormone receptor [HR] and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 [HER2] status), genomic risk, and patient preferences. All treatment decisions should be made through shared decision-making within a multidisciplinary tumor board. NCCN recognizes the importance of clinical trials and encourages participation when applicable and available.

nodes.label: Multidisciplinary Team Evaluation (including tumor board and shared decision-making)
Assess: Disease Extent, Tumor Biology (HR/HER2), Genomic Risk, Patient Factors
Curative Intent: Aggressive Multimodality Therapy (Surgery ± Radiation ± Systemic Therapy) to Eradicate All Disease
Adjuvant/Neoadjuvant Systemic Therapy to Eliminate Micrometastatic Disease and Downstage Tumors as Appropriate
Palliative Intent: Systemic Therapy Guided by Tumor Biology, Prior Therapy, and Patient Goals; Local Therapy for Symptom Control if Indicated
At Each Reassessment: Evaluate Clinical Benefit, Performance Status, and Patient Preferences via Shared Decision-Making
Continue Current or Next Line of Systemic Therapy

edges.label: Curative approach for all non-metastatic patients
Consider neoadjuvant/adjuvant systemic therapy based on tumor characteristics and surgical goals
Good performance status and continued clinical benefit
Compromised performance status or no therapeutic benefit

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Patient group: Patients with non-metastatic (stage 0, I, II, III) invasive or non-invasive breast cancer. Approach: Aggressive multimodality treatment aimed at eradicating all disease. This typically includes local therapy (surgery ± radiation) to achieve locoregional control and systemic therapy (endocrine, chemotherapy, targeted, immunotherapy) to reduce the risk of distant recurrence.

cards.title: Adjuvant/Neoadjuvant
cards.detail: Patient group: Patients with non-metastatic disease receiving systemic therapy after (adjuvant) or before (neoadjuvant) definitive local therapy. Approach: Systemic therapy is administered to eliminate micrometastatic disease and reduce recurrence risk. Neoadjuvant therapy may also be used to downstage tumors for breast conservation or to provide in vivo chemosensitivity information.

cards.title: Palliative
cards.detail: Patient group: Patients with recurrent, unresectable, or metastatic (stage IV) disease. Approach: Systemic therapy is administered to prolong survival, control symptoms, and maintain or improve quality of life. Treatment selection is guided by tumor biology, prior therapies, performance status, and patient goals. Local therapies (surgery, radiation) may be used for symptom control or in select oligometastatic scenarios.

cards.title: Multidisciplinary Team Requirements
cards.detail: The guidelines are developed and updated by a multidisciplinary panel of representatives from NCCN Member Institutions with expertise in medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, and reconstructive surgery. Optimal patient care requires a multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach for evaluation and treatment planning. A multidisciplinary approach is especially important in the management of breast cancer recurrence to consider all potential treatment options.

cards.title: Performance Status Guide
cards.detail: Patient performance status is a critical consideration, particularly in the palliative setting. Most patients with metastatic disease will be candidates for multiple lines of systemic therapy. At each reassessment, clinicians should assess the value of ongoing treatment, the risks and benefits of an additional line of systemic therapy, patient performance status, and patient preferences through a shared decision-making process. The potential side effects of additional lines of therapy may outweigh any clinical benefit in a patient who has a compromised performance status.
